strategy_summary: A compound released by Lactobacillus fermentum prevents binding by Staphylococcus aureus bacteria by outcompeting for the pathogen's tissue binding sites. “Applying a harmless bacterium or its products to surgical wounds may thwart infections by the dangerous pathogen Staphylococcus aureus, a major cause of…
mechanism:
A compound released by Lactobacillus fermentum prevents binding by Staphylococcus aureus bacteria by outcompeting for the pathogen's tissue binding sites.

“Applying a harmless bacterium or its products to surgical wounds may thwart infections by the dangerous pathogen Staphylococcus aureus, a major cause of hospital-acquired infections and one that grows more threatening as the incidence of antibiotic resistance rises…The bacterium, known as Lactobacillus fermentum, seems to exert at least part of its protective effects by secreting a protein that prevents S. aureus from binding to its target cells.” (Strauss 2000:2231-2233)
